title: The cost of avoiding risk entirely
subtitle: There is no zero-risk position. Holding cash carries inflation risk. Avoiding equities carries opportunity cost. Every choice has a price.

body.content: The most common misconception in personal finance is that keeping money in cash or savings accounts is risk-free. It is not. It trades market risk for inflation risk — and inflation risk is slower, quieter, and far less visible, which is why it is so consistently underestimated.

body.label: Inflation math
body.content: At 3% annual inflation, the purchasing power of $100,000 held in cash falls to approximately $74,000 in real terms after 10 years. No market crash required. No drama. Just time and the persistent erosion of value by a rate most people barely notice.

Opportunity cost is the second dimension. Every dollar kept in a 4% savings account, when equities have historically returned 7-10% annually over long periods, has a cost attached to it. That cost is real even if it never appears on a statement.

This is not an argument against holding cash. Cash has a specific and important role: emergency reserves, near-term planned expenses, and dry powder for opportunities. The error is holding excess cash beyond these purposes as a default state — treating risk avoidance as a strategy rather than as a temporary position.
Avoiding risk is itself a risk. The question is not whether to accept risk — it is which risks you are accepting and whether they are the ones you intended to take.
